name: Roman Power Center
description: Power center evolution from the Roman Empire (27 BCE) to the legal end of the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire in 1453. The Roman Republic (509-27 BCE) is not yet a separate entity in the DB — it is discussed in description only. The continuity from Western Rome to Byzantium is contested in Western historiography but Byzantines themselves used the demonym Ῥωμαῖοι (Romans).
ethical_notes: The 476 'fall of Rome' is a Western European historiographic frame: the Eastern Roman Empire continued for another 977 years. Calling that empire 'Byzantine' (a 16th-c. Western coinage) implies a discontinuity that its inhabitants did not recognize. Conversely, treating Ottoman conquest 1453 as a 'continuation' would be the opposite mistake.

sources.citation: Cambridge Ancient History vols. X-XIV; Cambridge Medieval History
Kaldellis, A. 'Romanland: Ethnicity and Empire in Byzantium' (2019)

links.description: Augustus assumes principate after civil wars (27 BCE). Formally 'res publica restituta' but de facto monarchical reform — the Republic framing is itself imperial propaganda.
Constantine refounds Byzantium as Constantinople; gradual transformation through Theodosius I division of the empire (395).

name: Chinese Imperial Dynasties
description: Major dynasties of imperial China (Han → Tang → Song → Yuan → Ming → Qing), with explicit transition_type per change. Sui (581-618) and intermediate dynasties omitted because not yet seeded; this is a highlighted trunk, not a complete dynastic tree.
ethical_notes: The traditional dynastic-cycle framework (heaven's mandate / 天命) is itself a piece of Chinese political theology. Transitions classified as REFORM or REVOLUTION here use modern historiographic categories; contemporaries would have framed the same events through 天命.

links.description: Li Yuan founds Tang after Sui collapse and civil war.
Mongol conquest (Kublai Khan); Song formally ends 1279.
links.ethical_notes: Mongol conquest involved mass casualties in northern China; exact estimates contested but Sichuan and Gansu populations collapsed dramatically.

links.description: Zhu Yuanzhang's Red Turban rebellion expels Mongols.

links.ethical_notes: Conquest accompanied by Yangzhou massacre (1645) and forced queue hairstyle (1645+) as sign of submission.

name: Tawantinsuyu → Virreinato del Perú
description: Inca empire → Spanish viceroyalty. Republic of Peru (1821-) is discussed in ethical_notes but not yet a separate entity in the DB, so it's not a link node.
ethical_notes: The Spanish 'conquest' of Tawantinsuyu (1532-72) involved smallpox-driven demographic collapse (50-90% population loss), execution of Atahualpa (1533) and Túpac Amaru I (1572), forced labor (mita), and systematic destruction of khipu records. The Peruvian Republic of 1821 was founded by criollo elites with limited indigenous representation; the Túpac Amaru II rebellion (1780-83) had been brutally suppressed. To call the colonial transition 'SUCCESSION' would be euphemism — hence COLONIAL chain with CONQUEST transition_type.

sources.citation: Hemming, J. 'The Conquest of the Incas' (1970/2003)
Mumford, J. 'Vertical Empire: The General Resettlement of Indians in the Colonial Andes' (2012)

links.description: Spanish viceroyalty formally established 1542 after a decade of conquest, civil wars, and Inca resistance.
links.ethical_notes: Pizarro's expedition reached Cajamarca 1532; Atahualpa executed 1533; Cusco sacked. The neo-Inca state at Vilcabamba resisted until 1572 when Túpac Amaru I was beheaded.

name: Sacrum Imperium Romanum → Deutsches Kaiserreich → Deutsches Reich
description: Self-proclaimed German imperial continuity. Each successor explicitly invoked the 'Reich' lineage.
ethical_notes: WARNING: this chain reflects SELF-PROCLAIMED ideological continuity, not legal/political legitimacy. The Holy Roman Empire (962-1806) was a multi-ethnic feudal/elective construct dominated but not exclusively German. The German Empire (1871-1918) consciously invoked 'Second Reich' framing to claim succession. The Third Reich (1933-45) claimed 'Tausendjähriges Reich' and weaponized the ideological lineage for racial-totalitarian ends; this chain is included precisely to expose how ideological continuity claims can be instrumentalized for genocide. Modern Germany (FRG) explicitly REJECTS the 'Reich' lineage.

sources.citation: Wilson, P. 'The Holy Roman Empire: A Thousand Years of Europe's History' (2016)
Evans, R. 'The Third Reich in Power' (2005)

links.description: Bismarck's unification via wars 1864/1866/1870-71; HRE had been dissolved 1806 — 65-year gap. The 'Second Reich' framing is retrospective.
links.ethical_notes: 1806→1871 gap means this is ideological invocation, not formal succession.

links.description: Nazi seizure of power; Weimar Republic skipped in this chain because the Nazis explicitly framed themselves as restoring 'Reich' lineage.
links.ethical_notes: The Holocaust (~6M Jews + ~5M others murdered), WWII (~70M deaths), and systematic atrocities (Generalplan Ost, Lebensborn) make this 'continuation' a moral obscenity. Included in the chain to make ideological appropriation visible, NOT to legitimize the lineage claim.

name: Ottoman → Republic of Turkey
description: Ottoman Empire dissolution after WWI (Sevres 1920, Lausanne 1923) into the Turkish Republic. Mandate-era Arab states are successor states but not included here pending richer entity coverage.
ethical_notes: The 'successor states' frame elides the Sykes-Picot 1916 colonial division of Arab lands by France/UK. The Treaty of Lausanne 1923 also legitimized the post-Armenian-Genocide demographic reality (1915-23 Armenian, Greek, Assyrian genocides ~1.5M+ killed). Republic of Turkey continues to deny the Armenian Genocide as such — this is itself a piece of ETHICS-008 silenced history.

sources.citation: Findley, C. 'Turkey, Islam, Nationalism, and Modernity' (2010)
Akçam, T. 'A Shameful Act: The Armenian Genocide and the Question of Turkish Responsibility' (2006)

links.description: Atatürk's Turkish War of Independence; sultanate abolished 1922, republic proclaimed 1923 (Lausanne).
links.ethical_notes: Foundational era includes the conclusion of Armenian/Greek/Assyrian genocides (1915-23) and the Greco-Turkish population exchange (~1.6M displaced).

name: Российская Империя → СССР → Российская Федерация
description: Russian state continuity through the 1917 revolution and 1991 dissolution.
ethical_notes: The USSR 'continuity' from the Russian Empire is contested: Soviets explicitly REJECTED the Tsarist legacy ideologically while inheriting most of its territory and great-power posture. The Russian Federation 1991 was recognized as the USSR's juridical continuator at the UN (kept Security Council seat, debt obligations, treaty commitments) — but other 14 republics were treated as successor states, not continuators. Calling this a 'restoration' chain is itself contested.

sources.citation: Pipes, R. 'Russia under the Bolshevik Regime' (1994)

links.description: Russian Revolution (Feb/Oct 1917) → civil war (1918-22) → USSR formally constituted Dec 1922.
links.ethical_notes: Civil war ~7-12M deaths; 1921-22 famine ~5M; Red Terror; Polish-Soviet War 1919-21. The 'continuity' includes brutal Sovietization of non-Russian republics (Caucasus, Central Asia, Ukraine).

links.description: Belovezha Accords Dec 1991 dissolved USSR. Russia recognized as juridical continuator (UN seat, debts, treaties).
